---
title: "Global Cybersecurity Threats"
subtitle: "2015–2024 · Incident Patterns Across Countries, Industries & Attack Vectors"
author: "Vithujan Shanmugaratnam · Francis Lehner · Brian Bernhauser"
date: "01.06.2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: dark
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    slide-number: c/t
    footer: "VDSS | Global Cybersecurity Threat Analysis 2015–2024"
    fig-align: center
    embed-resources: true
    progress: true
    code-fold: true
    highlight-style: github-dark
    width: 1280
    height: 720
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{=html}
<style>
/* ── Typography & spacing ───────────────────────────── */
.reveal h1, .reveal h2 { letter-spacing: -0.02em; }
.reveal h1 { font-size: 2.0em; }
.reveal h2 { font-size: 1.4em; border-bottom: 2px solid #4FC3F7; padding-bottom: 0.15em; }
.reveal section { font-size: 0.92em; }

/* ── KPI card grid ──────────────────────────────────── */
.kpi-grid {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr);
  gap: 1rem;
  margin-top: 1.2rem;
}
.kpi-card {
  background: #1e293b;
  border-radius: 10px;
  padding: 1.2rem 1rem;
  text-align: center;
  border-left: 4px solid;
}
.kpi-val  { font-size: 2.0em; font-weight: 700; display: block; line-height: 1.1; }
.kpi-lbl  { font-size: 0.78em; opacity: 0.7;  display: block; margin-top: 0.3em; }

/* ── Persona cards ──────────────────────────────────── */
.persona-quote {
  background: #1e293b;
  border-left: 4px solid #4FC3F7;
  border-radius: 6px;
  padding: 0.8rem 1rem;
  font-style: italic;
  margin: 0.8rem 0;
}

/* ── Agenda ─────────────────────────────────────────── */
.agenda-item {
  display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 0.8rem;
  padding: 0.4rem 0;
}
.agenda-num {
  background: #4FC3F7; color: #000;
  border-radius: 50%; width: 1.6em; height: 1.6em;
  display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
  font-weight: 700; font-size: 0.9em; flex-shrink: 0;
}

/* ── Section divider accent ─────────────────────────── */
.reveal section.center h1 { border-bottom: 3px solid #4FC3F7; display: inline-block; padding-bottom: 0.2em; }
</style>
```

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false
#| output: false
import warnings, os
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
import matplotlib
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np
from pathlib import Path

# ── Robust CSV resolution ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Quarto sets cwd to the document directory; fall back to parent/data/raw.
_cwd = Path(os.getcwd())
for _candidate in [
    _cwd / "Global_Cybersecurity_Threats_2015-2024.csv",
    _cwd / "data" / "raw" / "Global_Cybersecurity_Threats_2015-2024.csv",
    _cwd.parent / "data" / "raw" / "Global_Cybersecurity_Threats_2015-2024.csv",
]:
    if _candidate.exists():
        _csv_path = _candidate
        break
else:
    raise FileNotFoundError(
        "CSV not found. Place 'Global_Cybersecurity_Threats_2015-2024.csv' "
        "in the same folder as the .qmd file, or in data/raw/ within the project."
    )

# ── Load & normalise columns ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
df = pd.read_csv(_csv_path)
df.columns = (
    df.columns.str.strip().str.lower()
    .str.replace(r"[\s/]+", "_", regex=True)
    .str.replace(r"[^\w]", "",  regex=True)
)

# Friendly column aliases used throughout
COL = dict(
    loss   = "financial_loss_in_million_",
    users  = "number_of_affected_users",
    time   = "incident_resolution_time_in_hours",
    attack = "attack_type",
    vuln   = "security_vulnerability_type",
    defense= "defense_mechanism_used",
    src    = "attack_source",
    ind    = "target_industry",
    ctry   = "country",
    year   = "year",
)

# ── Consistent dark-theme colour palette ─────────────────────────────────────
BG    = "#191919"
BG2   = "#1e293b"   # card bg
C = dict(
    blue   = "#4FC3F7",
    orange = "#FF8A65",
    green  = "#A5D6A7",
    red    = "#EF9A9A",
    yellow = "#FFF176",
    purple = "#CE93D8",
    teal   = "#80DEEA",
)
CAT7   = [C["blue"], C["orange"], C["green"], C["red"], C["yellow"], C["purple"], C["teal"]]
CAT6   = CAT7[:6]
CAT5   = CAT7[:5]

matplotlib.rcParams.update({
    "figure.facecolor": BG,
    "axes.facecolor":   BG,
    "text.color":       "white",
    "axes.labelcolor":  "white",
    "xtick.color":      "white",
    "ytick.color":      "white",
    "axes.edgecolor":   "#555555",
    "grid.color":       "#333333",
    "axes.grid":        True,
    "axes.grid.axis":   "x",
    "grid.alpha":       0.3,
    "font.family":      "sans-serif",
    "font.size":        12,
    "axes.titlesize":   14,
    "axes.labelsize":   12,
    "figure.dpi":       130,
})

def clean_axes(ax, grid_axis="x"):
    """Remove top/right spines and set subtle grid."""
    ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines["left"].set_color("#555555")
    ax.spines["bottom"].set_color("#555555")
    ax.grid(True, axis=grid_axis, alpha=0.25, linewidth=0.8)
    ax.set_axisbelow(True)

# ── Pre-compute aggregations (fast reuse across slides) ───────────────────────
# KPI scalars
kpi_total     = len(df)
kpi_avg_loss  = df[COL["loss"]].mean()
kpi_avg_time  = df[COL["time"]].mean()
kpi_top_atk   = df[COL["attack"]].mode()[0]
kpi_tot_loss  = df[COL["loss"]].sum()

# Temporal
yearly = df.groupby(COL["year"]).agg(
    incidents = (COL["ctry"], "count"),
    avg_loss  = (COL["loss"],  "mean"),
    avg_users = (COL["users"], "mean"),
    avg_time  = (COL["time"],  "mean"),
).reset_index()

# By industry
ind_metrics = df.groupby(COL["ind"]).agg(
    incidents = (COL["ind"],   "count"),
    avg_loss  = (COL["loss"],  "mean"),
    avg_users = (COL["users"], "mean"),
    avg_time  = (COL["time"],  "mean"),
).reset_index()

# By country
ctry_metrics = df.groupby(COL["ctry"]).agg(
    incidents = (COL["ctry"], "count"),
    avg_loss  = (COL["loss"], "mean"),
).reset_index()

# Heatmap: attack type × industry
heat_atk_ind = df.pivot_table(
    index=COL["attack"], columns=COL["ind"],
    values=COL["loss"], aggfunc="mean"
)

# Defense outcomes (sorted by median loss)
defense_order = (df.groupby(COL["defense"])[COL["loss"]]
                   .median().sort_values(ascending=True).index.tolist())
defense_data  = [df[df[COL["defense"]] == d][COL["loss"]].values
                 for d in defense_order]

# Attack & vulnerability distributions
atk_counts  = df[COL["attack"]].value_counts().sort_values()
vuln_counts = df[COL["vuln"]].value_counts().sort_values()
```

## Agenda

<div style="margin-top:1.2rem;">
<div class="agenda-item"><div class="agenda-num">1</div><span>**Problem & Solution** — Why this project?</span></div>
<div class="agenda-item"><div class="agenda-num">2</div><span>**Audience** — Who are our users?</span></div>
<div class="agenda-item"><div class="agenda-num">3</div><span>**Data Source** — Dataset overview & acquisition</span></div>
<div class="agenda-item"><div class="agenda-num">4</div><span>**Exploratory Analysis** — What the data reveals</span></div>
<div class="agenda-item"><div class="agenda-num">5</div><span>**Visualisation Product** — Design decisions & live examples</span></div>
<div class="agenda-item"><div class="agenda-num">6</div><span>**Key Findings & Summary**</span></div>
</div>

::: notes
Welcome everyone. We'll move through six sections today, each building on the previous one. The last section shows actual charts from the Quarto site we built.
:::

# 1 · Problem & Solution {.center}

## The Problem

**Cybersecurity teams drown in raw data — not insight.**

. . .

- Incident tables grow to thousands of rows per reporting cycle
- Comparing *frequency* vs. *severity* requires manual aggregation
- Cross-dimensional patterns (country × industry × attack type) stay hidden
- Every reporting cycle: export CSV → build charts in Excel → assemble slides → repeat

. . .

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"}
**Today's reality:** Analysts spend hours on mechanics, not analysis.
:::

::: notes
The core tension: data is abundant, insight is scarce. The manual workflow is not only slow — it introduces errors every cycle.
:::

## What We Built

An **interactive, reproducible web report** turning 3,000 raw incidents into navigable insight:

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Five analytical sections:**

- Headline KPIs & scale-setting
- Temporal trends (2015–2024)
- Country & industry comparisons
- Attack type & vulnerability profiles
- Defense mechanism outcomes
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Technical stack:**

- Single **Quarto** document → HTML report
- Python (pandas · matplotlib · seaborn)
- Published on **GitHub Pages** — no install required
- Fully reproducible from a fresh session
:::
::::

::: notes
One Quarto source file renders the entire report. Any team member can re-run it after a new data export and the site updates automatically.
:::

# 2 · Audience {.center}

## Persona 1 — Sophia, Senior Cybersecurity Analyst

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}
**Profile**

- Age 34 · Security Operations Centre
- Prepares trend analysis and internal reports regularly
- **High** domain expertise and data literacy
- 27″ desktop monitor — happy to explore deeply

<div class="persona-quote">
"I need to see which vulnerability types are most exploited in Banking — and whether that pattern differs from Healthcare."
</div>
:::
::: {.column width="42%"}
**Jobs to be done**

- Analyse attack trends by year, country, industry
- Compare vulnerabilities and defense outcomes
- Produce evidence-based briefings

**Pain points**

- Combining static charts is time-consuming
- Existing summaries lack multi-dimensional comparison
- Hard to separate frequent threats from severe ones
:::
::::

## Persona 2 — Marcus, IT Security Manager

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}
**Profile**

- Age 48 · Responsible for security priorities at management level
- Communicates risk to senior leadership
- **Medium** data literacy — limited time for exploration
- 14″ corporate laptop — needs fast time-to-insight

<div class="persona-quote">
"Show me the most affected industries and the biggest financial losses. I need to justify our budget priorities."
</div>
:::
::: {.column width="42%"}
**Jobs to be done**

- Quick overview of key threat patterns
- Identify high-risk sectors and attack types
- Communicate findings to executive stakeholders

**Pain points**

- Complex dashboards create friction and reduce trust
- Technical views don't translate to management communication
- Can't distinguish frequency from severity at a glance
:::
::::

::: notes
Marcus and Sophia pull in opposite directions on depth vs. speed. The report resolves this tension through a layered design: overview on load, detail on drill-down.
:::

# 3 · Data Source {.center}

## Kaggle — Global Cybersecurity Threats (2015–2024)

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**What is it?**

A public dataset of **3,000 cybersecurity incidents** spanning 2015–2024, published on Kaggle under a CC0 licence.

**Scope**

- **10 countries:** AUS · BRA · CHN · FRA · DEU · IND · JPN · RUS · GBR · USA
- **7 industries:** Banking · Education · Government · Healthcare · IT · Retail · Telecom
- **10 variables** per incident — no missing values, no imputation required
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}

| Variable | Role |
|---|---|
| Country, Year | Context |
| Attack Type, Attack Source | Threat |
| Target Industry, Vulnerability | Target |
| Defense Mechanism | Response |
| Financial Loss (M$) | Severity |
| Affected Users | Severity |
| Resolution Time (h) | Severity |

:::
::::

::: notes
The dataset is synthetic and evenly balanced — no country or industry dominates raw counts. This means cross-category comparisons are meaningful, and we can describe patterns without worrying about sampling bias.
:::

## Data Acquisition & Pipeline

**Automated download via `data_acquisition/load.py`:**

```python
import kaggle
kaggle.api.dataset_download_files(
    "atharvasoundankar/global-cybersecurity-threats-2015-2024",
    path="data/raw/", unzip=True
)
```

- API credentials stored in `.env` — never committed to version control
- Column names normalised to `lowercase_snake_case` at load time
- Any team member can reproduce acquisition independently
- Zero missing values — no imputation or exclusion needed

# 4 · Exploratory Analysis {.center}

## At a Glance — Key Numbers

```{python}
#| label: fig-kpis
#| fig-cap: "Headline metrics across all 3,000 incidents."
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(12, 2.6))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

kpis = [
    (f"{kpi_total:,}",         "Total Incidents",           C["blue"]),
    (f"${kpi_avg_loss:.1f}M",  "Avg. Financial Loss",       C["orange"]),
    (f"{kpi_avg_time:.0f} h",  "Avg. Resolution Time",      C["red"]),
    (kpi_top_atk,              "Most Common Attack Type",    C["yellow"]),
]
for ax, (val, label, color) in zip(axes, kpis):
    ax.set_facecolor(BG2)
    for sp in ax.spines.values():
        sp.set_edgecolor(color)
        sp.set_linewidth(2)
    ax.text(0.5, 0.60, val,   ha="center", va="center",
            fontsize=20, color=color, fontweight="bold",
            transform=ax.transAxes, wrap=True)
    ax.text(0.5, 0.20, label, ha="center", va="center",
            fontsize=10, color="white", alpha=0.65,
            transform=ax.transAxes)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])

plt.tight_layout(pad=1.0)
plt.show()
```

::: notes
The numbers are not surprising in isolation, but they set scale. $49 M average loss and 49-hour average resolution time are the baselines our visualisations measure everything against.
:::

## Balanced Coverage — Country & Industry

```{python}
#| label: fig-eda-overview
#| fig-cap: "Incident counts by country (left) and industry (right). All categories are evenly distributed."
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4.2))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

# Country
cc = df[COL["ctry"]].value_counts().sort_values()
bars_c = axes[0].barh(cc.index, cc.values, color=C["blue"], edgecolor="none", height=0.65)
axes[0].set_title("Incidents by Country", pad=10)
axes[0].set_xlabel("Number of Incidents")
clean_axes(axes[0])
# Highlight max
max_c = cc.values.max()
for bar, v in zip(bars_c, cc.values):
    if v == max_c:
        bar.set_color(C["orange"])
    axes[0].text(v + 3, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
                 f"{v}", va="center", ha="left", fontsize=9, color="white", alpha=0.7)

# Industry
ic = df[COL["ind"]].value_counts().sort_values()
bars_i = axes[1].barh(ic.index, ic.values, color=C["teal"], edgecolor="none", height=0.65)
axes[1].set_title("Incidents by Industry", pad=10)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Number of Incidents")
clean_axes(axes[1])
max_i = ic.values.max()
for bar, v in zip(bars_i, ic.values):
    if v == max_i:
        bar.set_color(C["orange"])
    axes[1].text(v + 3, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
                 f"{v}", va="center", ha="left", fontsize=9, color="white", alpha=0.7)

plt.tight_layout(pad=1.5)
plt.show()
```

All categories are **evenly distributed** — the dataset is synthetic and balanced, making cross-category comparisons meaningful without sampling corrections.

## Three Independent Dimensions of Severity

```{python}
#| label: fig-eda-corr
#| fig-cap: "Pearson correlations between the three numerical severity measures."
corr = df[[COL["loss"], COL["users"], COL["time"]]].corr()
corr.index   = ["Financial\nLoss", "Affected\nUsers", "Resolution\nTime"]
corr.columns = ["Financial\nLoss", "Affected\nUsers", "Resolution\nTime"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.5, 4.5))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG)
ax.set_facecolor(BG)

mask = np.zeros_like(corr, dtype=bool)
np.fill_diagonal(mask, True)  # hide diagonal (always 1)

sns.heatmap(
    corr, annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap="RdBu_r", center=0,
    square=True, ax=ax, linewidths=1, linecolor="#333333",
    vmin=-1, vmax=1, mask=mask,
    cbar_kws={"shrink": 0.75, "label": "Pearson r"},
    annot_kws={"fontsize": 14, "fontweight": "bold"},
)
ax.set_title("Pearson Correlation — Severity Dimensions", pad=12, color="white")
ax.tick_params(colors="white")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Near-zero correlations confirm that **financial loss, affected users, and resolution time are independent** — each adds unique information and all three must be surfaced in the report.

## Attack Types & Vulnerability Distribution

```{python}
#| label: fig-eda-attack
#| fig-cap: "Frequency of attack types (left) and vulnerability types (right)."
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4.2))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

# Attack types
atk_colors = [C["orange"] if v == atk_counts.max() else C["blue"] for v in atk_counts.values]
axes[0].barh(atk_counts.index, atk_counts.values, color=atk_colors, edgecolor="none", height=0.65)
axes[0].set_title("Attack Types", pad=10)
axes[0].set_xlabel("Incident Count")
clean_axes(axes[0])
for i, v in enumerate(atk_counts.values):
    axes[0].text(v + 3, i, f"{v}", va="center", ha="left", fontsize=9, color="white", alpha=0.7)

# Vulnerability types
vuln_colors = [C["orange"] if v == vuln_counts.max() else C["red"] for v in vuln_counts.values]
axes[1].barh(vuln_counts.index, vuln_counts.values, color=vuln_colors, edgecolor="none", height=0.65)
axes[1].set_title("Security Vulnerability Types", pad=10)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Incident Count")
clean_axes(axes[1])
for i, v in enumerate(vuln_counts.values):
    axes[1].text(v + 3, i, f"{v}", va="center", ha="left", fontsize=9, color="white", alpha=0.7)

plt.tight_layout(pad=1.5)
plt.show()
```

# 5 · Visualisation Product {.center}

## Product Structure — From Overview to Detail

The report is divided into **five sections** supporting both quick orientation and deeper analysis:

| Section | Primary Content | User |
|---|---|---|
| **Overview** | KPI cards, headline totals, sparkline | Marcus |
| **Trends** | Incidents & severity over 2015–2024 | Both |
| **Countries & Industries** | Frequency and severity rankings | Both |
| **Threat Profiles** | Attack types, vulnerabilities, attack sources | Sophia |
| **Defense & Outcomes** | Mechanisms vs. resolution time & loss | Sophia |

::: notes
The overview-to-detail principle means Marcus gets his answer on page load, while Sophia can keep scrolling. No user has to wade through complexity they don't need.
:::

## Overview — Setting Scale Immediately

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="44%"}
**Design rationale**

- KPI cards answer "how big is the problem?" before any filtering
- Familiar management communication format for Marcus
- Sparkline gives temporal context without needing a full trend chart

**What's surfaced**

- Total incident count
- Average & total financial loss
- Average resolution time
- Most frequent attack type
:::
::: {.column width="56%"}
```{python}
#| label: fig-prod-kpi
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(6.2, 3.8))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG)
kpis2 = [
    (f"{kpi_total:,}",              "Total Incidents",        C["blue"]),
    (f"${kpi_tot_loss:,.0f}M",      "Total Financial Loss",   C["orange"]),
    (f"${kpi_avg_loss:.1f}M",       "Avg. Loss per Incident", C["red"]),
    (f"{kpi_avg_time:.0f} h",       "Avg. Resolution Time",   C["purple"]),
]
for ax, (val, label, color) in zip(axes.flatten(), kpis2):
    ax.set_facecolor(BG2)
    for sp in ax.spines.values():
        sp.set_edgecolor(color); sp.set_linewidth(2)
    ax.text(0.5, 0.60, val, ha="center", va="center",
            fontsize=17, color=color, fontweight="bold", transform=ax.transAxes)
    ax.text(0.5, 0.18, label, ha="center", va="center",
            fontsize=9, color="white", alpha=0.65, transform=ax.transAxes)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
plt.tight_layout(pad=0.8)
plt.show()
```
:::
::::

## Trends — How Threats Evolved Over a Decade

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="44%"}
**Why line charts?**

- Time-series data maps naturally to connected lines
- Filled area emphasises cumulative scale
- Metric toggle (count / loss / users / time) avoids chart overload while keeping all severity dimensions accessible

**Design choice:** keep one metric visible at a time to prevent cognitive overload
:::
::: {.column width="56%"}
```{python}
#| label: fig-prod-trend
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.5, 4))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG)
ax.set_facecolor(BG)

ax.plot(yearly[COL["year"]], yearly["incidents"],
        color=C["blue"], linewidth=2.5, marker="o",
        markersize=6, markerfacecolor=C["orange"], markeredgewidth=0)
ax.fill_between(yearly[COL["year"]], yearly["incidents"],
                alpha=0.12, color=C["blue"])

# Annotate min and max
idx_max = yearly["incidents"].idxmax()
idx_min = yearly["incidents"].idxmin()
for idx, va, offset in [(idx_max, "bottom", 4), (idx_min, "top", -4)]:
    ax.annotate(
        f'{yearly.loc[idx, "incidents"]}',
        xy=(yearly.loc[idx, COL["year"]], yearly.loc[idx, "incidents"]),
        xytext=(0, offset), textcoords="offset points",
        ha="center", va=va, fontsize=10,
        color=C["orange"] if va == "bottom" else C["red"],
        fontweight="bold"
    )

ax.set_title("Cybersecurity Incidents per Year", pad=10)
ax.set_xlabel("Year")
ax.set_ylabel("Incident Count")
ax.set_xticks(yearly[COL["year"]])
ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=45)
clean_axes(ax, grid_axis="y")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```
:::
::::

## Countries & Industries — Ranking What Matters

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="44%"}
**Why sorted bars — not a map?**

- Dataset contains only 10 countries → precise ranking outweighs geography
- Sorted bars make order unambiguous at a glance
- A choropleth would add visual complexity without analytical gain

**Metric toggle:** count / loss / users / resolution time lets Marcus and Sophia pull the same chart for different answers
:::
::: {.column width="56%"}
```{python}
#| label: fig-prod-industry
ind_sorted = ind_metrics.sort_values("avg_loss")
bar_colors = [C["orange"] if v == ind_sorted["avg_loss"].max() else C["blue"]
              for v in ind_sorted["avg_loss"]]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.5, 4))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG)
ax.set_facecolor(BG)

ax.barh(ind_sorted[COL["ind"]], ind_sorted["avg_loss"],
        color=bar_colors, edgecolor="none", height=0.65)
for i, v in enumerate(ind_sorted["avg_loss"]):
    ax.text(v + 0.4, i, f"${v:.1f}M", va="center", ha="left",
            fontsize=9, color="white", alpha=0.75)

ax.set_title("Average Financial Loss by Industry", pad=10)
ax.set_xlabel("Avg. Loss (M$)")
clean_axes(ax, grid_axis="x")
ax.spines["left"].set_visible(False)
ax.tick_params(left=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```
:::
::::

::: notes
Sophia uses this chart by industry. Marcus uses it to justify budget allocation toward the hardest-hit sectors.
:::

## Threat Profiles — Concentration at a Glance

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="44%"}
**Why heatmaps?**

- Encode two categorical dimensions (attack × industry) without requiring separate charts per combination
- Colour intensity immediately highlights concentration
- Answers Sophia's question: *"Which vulnerability types are most common in Banking?"*

**No causal claims** — dataset is synthetic; patterns are described as within-data observations
:::
::: {.column width="56%"}
```{python}
#| label: fig-prod-heatmap
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 4.2))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG)
ax.set_facecolor(BG)

# Custom colourmap that respects dark background
cmap = sns.color_palette("YlOrRd", as_cmap=True)
sns.heatmap(
    heat_atk_ind, ax=ax,
    cmap=cmap, annot=True, fmt=".0f",
    linewidths=0.8, linecolor="#222222",
    cbar_kws={"label": "Avg. Loss (M$)", "shrink": 0.85},
    annot_kws={"fontsize": 9, "color": "black"},
)
ax.set_title("Avg. Financial Loss: Attack Type × Industry", pad=10, color="white")
ax.set_xlabel("")
ax.set_ylabel("")
ax.tick_params(colors="white")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```
:::
::::

## Defense & Outcomes — Do Defenses Make a Difference?

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="44%"}
**Why box plots?**

- Distributions reveal spread and outliers — not just averages
- Avoids misleading summary statistics when underlying data is wide
- Sorted by median → ranking is immediately visible

**Key question for Sophia:** Are mechanisms like AI-based Detection or Encryption genuinely associated with lower losses — or is it noise?
:::
::: {.column width="56%"}
```{python}
#| label: fig-prod-boxplot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.5, 4))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG)
ax.set_facecolor(BG)

bp = ax.boxplot(
    defense_data, vert=False, patch_artist=True,
    medianprops=dict(color="white", linewidth=2.5),
    boxprops=dict(linewidth=0),
    whiskerprops=dict(color="#888888", linewidth=1.2),
    capprops=dict(color="#888888", linewidth=1.2),
    flierprops=dict(marker="o", markerfacecolor="#555555",
                    markersize=3, markeredgewidth=0, alpha=0.4),
)
for patch, color in zip(bp["boxes"], CAT5):
    patch.set_facecolor(color)
    patch.set_alpha(0.75)

ax.set_yticks(range(1, len(defense_order) + 1))
ax.set_yticklabels(defense_order)
ax.set_title("Financial Loss Distribution by Defense Mechanism", pad=10)
ax.set_xlabel("Financial Loss (M$)")
clean_axes(ax, grid_axis="x")
ax.spines["left"].set_visible(False)
ax.tick_params(left=False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```
:::
::::

::: notes
The overlapping distributions suggest the dataset's synthetic nature — real-world data would likely show sharper separation. We annotate this caveat directly in the report.
:::

## Design Decisions — Summary

| Decision | Rationale |
|---|---|
| **Sorted bar charts** over choropleth maps | Only 10 countries — ranking beats geography |
| **Metric toggle** (count / loss / users / time) | Explicit frequency vs. severity distinction; avoids chart overload |
| **Box plots** for defense outcomes | Distributions are more honest than means for skewed severity data |
| **Heatmaps** for cross-category analysis | Compact, two-dimensional categorical relationships |
| **Overview → detail** structure | Fast entry for Marcus; depth for Sophia |
| **No causal claims** in annotations | Dataset is synthetic — findings described as within-data patterns |
| **GitHub Pages deployment** | Reproducible, version-controlled, no installation required |

# 6 · Key Findings & Summary {.center}

## What the Data Tells Us

```{python}
#| label: fig-summary
#| fig-cap: "Top: incident trend over time. Bottom-left: loss by industry. Bottom-right: defense outcome distributions."
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5.5))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(BG)
gs = fig.add_gridspec(2, 2, hspace=0.45, wspace=0.35)

# ── Trend ────────────────────────────────────────────
ax1 = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, :])
ax1.set_facecolor(BG)
ax1.plot(yearly[COL["year"]], yearly["avg_loss"],
         color=C["orange"], linewidth=2.5, marker="o", markersize=5,
         markerfacecolor="white", markeredgewidth=0)
ax1.fill_between(yearly[COL["year"]], yearly["avg_loss"], alpha=0.12, color=C["orange"])
ax1.set_title("Average Financial Loss per Year (M$)", pad=8)
ax1.set_xlabel(""); ax1.set_xticks(yearly[COL["year"]])
ax1.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=45, labelsize=9)
clean_axes(ax1, grid_axis="y")

# ── Industry loss ─────────────────────────────────────
ax2 = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 0])
ax2.set_facecolor(BG)
ind_s = ind_metrics.sort_values("avg_loss")
bar_c2 = [C["orange"] if v == ind_s["avg_loss"].max() else C["blue"]
           for v in ind_s["avg_loss"]]
ax2.barh(ind_s[COL["ind"]], ind_s["avg_loss"], color=bar_c2, edgecolor="none", height=0.6)
ax2.set_title("Avg. Loss by Industry (M$)", pad=8, fontsize=12)
ax2.set_xlabel("M$", fontsize=10)
clean_axes(ax2, grid_axis="x")
ax2.tick_params(labelsize=9)

# ── Defense box ───────────────────────────────────────
ax3 = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 1])
ax3.set_facecolor(BG)
bp2 = ax3.boxplot(
    defense_data, vert=False, patch_artist=True,
    medianprops=dict(color="white", linewidth=2),
    boxprops=dict(linewidth=0),
    whiskerprops=dict(color="#888888"), capprops=dict(color="#888888"),
    flierprops=dict(marker="o", markerfacecolor="#555555",
                    markersize=2, markeredgewidth=0, alpha=0.3),
)
for patch, color in zip(bp2["boxes"], CAT5):
    patch.set_facecolor(color); patch.set_alpha(0.75)
ax3.set_yticks(range(1, len(defense_order) + 1))
ax3.set_yticklabels(defense_order, fontsize=9)
ax3.set_title("Loss by Defense Mechanism", pad=8, fontsize=12)
ax3.set_xlabel("M$", fontsize=10)
clean_axes(ax3, grid_axis="x")

plt.show()
```

::: notes
The three panels together tell the story: loss is stable year-on-year (no clear escalation), varies moderately by industry, and distributions overlap heavily across defense types — a finding worth investigating with richer, real-world data.
:::

## Summary & Next Steps

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**What we delivered**

- A **single-source, reproducible** Quarto report rendering to GitHub Pages
- Five analytical sections covering trend, geography, industry, threat, and defense
- Dual-persona design: fast overview for Marcus, depth for Sophia
- All charts use consistent, accessible colour coding and honest distribution displays

**Key data findings**

- Severity dimensions (loss, users, time) are statistically independent
- All countries and industries share similar incident exposure
- Defense mechanism outcomes overlap widely in this synthetic dataset
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**If extended with real data:**

- Replace balanced synthetic records with live SIEM exports
- Add interactive filters (Plotly / Observable) for sector-specific drilldown
- Introduce time-lagged correlations between attack volume and financial impact
- Automate periodic refresh via GitHub Actions

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**Reproducibility:** The entire report renders from a fresh Python environment with a single `quarto render` command.
:::
:::
::::

---

## {.center}

<br>

**Global Cybersecurity Threats 2015–2024**

*A Visual Data Story for Security Teams*

<br>

Vithujan Shanmugaratnam · Francis Lehner · Brian Bernhauser

**ZHAW — VDSS Project 2026**

<br>

::: {style="font-size:0.8em; opacity:0.55;"}
Data: Kaggle CC0 · Built with Quarto + Python · Source on GitHub
:::
